turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: In case 16-1094, the Republic of Sudan v. Harrison, Justice Alito has the opinion of the Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: This case, which comes to us from the United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit, concerns the methods that a party may use in serving process on a foreign state. The plaintiffs in this case sued the Republic of Sudan claiming that it was involved in the bombing of the U.S.S. Cole. They attempted to serve Sudan by sending a mailing address to the Foreign Minister of Sudan at the Sudanese Embassy here in Washington. The Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act of 1976 sets out the only methods of service on a foreign state that are allowed. One authorized method is mailing process addressed to a nation's Minister of Foreign Affairs. But Foreign Ministers do not customarily work at and only very occasionally visit their nation's embassies. We therefore conclude that service by mailing process to an embassy is not authorized by the Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act and this compels us to reverse the decision below. This decision does not mean that the plaintiffs' attempts to recover from Sudan are over, but it does mean that they must now serve process by one of the methods allowed by law. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion.
